title: How to File Amazon Shipment Discrepancy Claims
excerpt: You sent 500 units; Amazon received 488. Here is how to catch shorted shipments and recover the units that never got checked in.

blocks.text: A shipment discrepancy is the gap between what you sent to a fulfillment center and what Amazon recorded as received. Sometimes missing units arrive late and reconcile on their own. Sometimes they don't — and that's owed inventory you can claim, if you catch it in time.

Not every mismatch is claimable, and not immediately. Amazon gives received quantities time to catch up as units are processed across the network. The claimable cases are the ones that remain short after that reconciliation period closes.

cells: Units received < units shipped, after reconciliation window
Units still being checked in (within window)
Carrier lost the shipment in transit

items.title: Keep proof of what you sent
items.text: Retain your shipment plan, packing list, and carrier documentation. Without proof of shipped quantity, you cannot substantiate a claim.

items.title: Wait out the reconciliation window
items.text: Give Amazon the stated time to finish receiving before treating a shortage as final.

items.title: Pull the reconciliation view
items.text: Compare shipped vs. received per SKU and identify what remains short.

items.title: Open a claim with evidence
items.text: Attach your packing list and carrier proof. Be specific about SKU and quantity.

items.title: Track to resolution
items.text: Follow up if the case stalls; note the response for your records.

blocks.title: Mind the clock
blocks.text: With Amazon's shorter claim windows, an annual reconciliation will miss valid shortages. Check inbound shipments monthly so nothing expires unclaimed.

blocks.title: Make it routine
blocks.text: Fold shipment reconciliation into the same monthly session you use for reimbursements. The data lives in adjacent reports, so doing them together saves time.
For sellers with steady inbound volume, reconciliation tooling automates the matching and flags shortages automatically — worth it once the manual check outgrows a quick monthly pass.
